event: The Cucuteni-Trypillia culture flourishes across the region, leaving behind large agricultural settlements and distinctive painted pottery.
Thracian and Getae tribes settle the lands between the Prut and Dniester rivers.
The Persian king Darius I campaigns near the lower Danube, bringing the Getae of the region into recorded history.
Greek and later Roman trade influence reaches the region through colonies on the nearby Black Sea coast.
Rome conquers Dacia under Emperor Trajan, drawing the surrounding lands, including future Moldova, into the Roman cultural sphere.
Rome withdraws from Dacia, after which the region sees successive waves of migrating peoples.
The Huns sweep through the region, displacing the Goths who had settled the lands north of the Danube.
Slavic peoples migrate into and settle across the territory during the early medieval period.
The Mongol invasion of Europe devastates the region, which falls under the influence of the Golden Horde.
Voivode Bogdan I establishes the independent Principality of Moldavia, breaking free from Hungarian overlordship.
Stephen the Great ascends the throne of Moldavia, beginning a long reign marked by military victories and cultural patronage.
Stephen the Great defeats a large Ottoman army at the Battle of Vaslui, briefly halting Ottoman expansion.
Moldavia becomes a tributary vassal state of the Ottoman Empire, retaining internal autonomy under its own princes.
Michael the Brave briefly unites Wallachia, Transylvania, and Moldavia, foreshadowing later ideas of Romanian unity.
Prince Dimitrie Cantemir allies Moldavia with Russia against the Ottomans but is defeated at the Battle of Stănilești.
By the Treaty of Bucharest, the Ottomans cede the eastern half of Moldavia, Bessarabia, to the Russian Empire.
The western part of historic Moldavia unites with Wallachia under Alexandru Ioan Cuza to form the basis of modern Romania.
Russia regains southern Bessarabia from Romania following the Russo-Turkish War and the Congress of Berlin.
Amid the Russian Revolution, the National Council, Sfatul Țării, convenes and proclaims the Moldavian Democratic Republic.
The Sfatul Țării votes for the union of Bessarabia with the Kingdom of Romania.
The Soviet Union creates the Moldavian Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic on the east bank of the Dniester within Ukraine.
The USSR annexes Bessarabia under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact and establishes the Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic.
Romanian and German forces reoccupy Bessarabia during the invasion of the Soviet Union, leading to mass deportations and killings of Jews.
The Red Army retakes the region and reestablishes Soviet control over the Moldavian SSR.
A severe famine, worsened by Soviet grain requisitions, kills tens of thousands across the Moldavian SSR.
Mass deportations of peasants and perceived dissidents to Siberia accompany the forced collectivization of agriculture.
Moldovan is made the official state language using the Latin script, sparking ethnic tensions and nationalist mobilization.
Separatists proclaim the breakaway Transnistria republic east of the Dniester, and Gagauz autonomy is also declared in the south.
Moldova declares independence from the Soviet Union following the failed August coup in Moscow.
A short but deadly war over Transnistria ends with a ceasefire, leaving the region a Russian-backed frozen conflict zone.
Moldova is admitted as a member of the United Nations.
Moldova adopts a new constitution, declaring permanent neutrality and granting special autonomous status to Gagauzia.
Moldova joins the Council of Europe, committing to democratic and human-rights standards.
The Party of Communists wins parliamentary elections, and Vladimir Voronin becomes the first communist head of state in post-Soviet Europe.
Moldova enters the European Neighbourhood Policy, deepening cooperation with the European Union.
Disputed elections trigger the 'Twitter Revolution' protests, leading to the fall of the Communist government and a pro-European coalition.
Moldova signs an Association Agreement and Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area deal with the European Union.
Around one billion dollars, an eighth of GDP, vanishes from Moldovan banks in a massive fraud scandal.
Pro-Russian candidate Igor Dodon wins the first direct presidential election held since 1996.
Maia Sandu wins the presidency, becoming Moldova's first female head of state on a pro-European, anti-corruption platform.
Moldova is granted European Union candidate status amid the regional shock of Russia's invasion of neighbouring Ukraine.
Moldova hosts the second European Political Community summit, signaling its growing integration with Europe.
Maia Sandu wins reelection and a referendum narrowly approves enshrining the goal of EU membership in the constitution.
The European Union formally opens accession negotiations with Moldova.
